metadata.title: Why a Blinding Red Lamp Releases Fewer Electrons Than a Dim UV Torch
metadata.color_semantics: TEAL = photon above threshold / electron ejected; CRIMSON = photon below threshold / no electron
metadata.note: Exclusions: no stopping-potential algebra, no Einstein-equation derivation, no Compton scattering, no Millikan biography beyond one spoken line.

beats.narration_text: A million-watt red spotlight aimed at sodium metal releases exactly zero electrons. A pocket UV penlight releases them instantly. The difference is not power. It is frequency.

card.copy: Why a Blinding Red Lamp Releases Fewer Electrons Than a Dim UV Torch

beats.narration_text: Three experimental facts. One: below a threshold frequency, no electrons — ever, at any intensity. Two: above threshold, electrons emerge immediately, with no time delay. Three: the kinetic energy of each electron depends only on the frequency, not the intensity.

Classical wave theory says energy is delivered uniformly across the surface. Crank up the intensity and eventually any frequency should free an electron. So why is there a sharp threshold? Why does a dim UV source work when a blinding red lamp fails?

card.copy:
Classical waves: more intensity = more energy.
Below threshold: zero electrons at any intensity.
Why?

beats.narration_text: Einstein's 1905 answer: light energy is not spread continuously. It arrives as discrete packets — photons — each carrying exactly h times nu. One photon hits one electron. If that photon's energy is less than the work function phi, the electron cannot escape, period.

The equation is kinetic energy equals h-nu minus phi. Above threshold: the electron escapes with kinetic energy proportional to how far above threshold you are. Below threshold: h-nu minus phi is negative — and there is no such thing as negative kinetic energy. No escape.

For sodium, the work function is 2.28 electron volts. Green light at 546 nanometers carries 2.27 electron volts per photon — one hundredth of a volt below threshold. Result: zero electrons from a ten-thousand-watt lamp. A UV photon at 300 nanometers carries 4.13 electron volts — nearly two volts above threshold. Each photon ejects one electron.

The key point: photons cannot pool their energy. A thousand red photons arriving one after another cannot combine to free one electron the way that waves could. Each photon acts alone, and a sub-threshold photon fails alone.

Intensity controls only how many photons arrive per second — the rate. Above threshold: more photons means more electrons, but the same kinetic energy per electron. Intensity is a count; frequency is the energy. They are independent.

Millikan tried for years to disprove this. His precise measurements of the stopping potential for sodium, lithium, and potassium confirmed Einstein's equation exactly. He gave the Nobel Committee his h measurement and his personal disbelief. Einstein won the 1921 Nobel Prize specifically for this paper.

One photon. One electron. Threshold frequency determines escape. Intensity determines count, not energy. Red lamp: sub-threshold photons, zero electrons. UV torch: above threshold, immediate emission.

card.copy:
KE = hν − φ. Below threshold: no electrons ever.
Intensity = count, not energy.
One photon acts alone.

beats.narration_text: Part of the Quantum Mechanics: Foundations: The Quantum World series.
